title: Monetary Policy Report—October 2024
summary: The Bank of Canada reduced its target for the overnight rate by 50 basis points to 3¾% (Bank Rate at 4%, deposit rate at 3¾%), with CPI inflation now around the 2% target within their 1-3% control range. The Canadian economy remains in excess supply, with a softened labor market where unemployment increases are concentrated among newcomers and youth, while wage growth remains elevated relative to productivity. Canada's economic growth is forecast to average 2¼% over 2025 and 2026.
key_points: Bank of Canada cut policy rate by 50 bps to 3¾% (overnight target), with Bank Rate at 4% and deposit rate at 3¾%
CPI inflation is now around 2%, within the Bank's control range of 1% to 3%
Canadian economy continues to be in excess supply; GDP per person is declining
Labor market has softened with unemployment increases concentrated among newcomers and youth
Wage growth remains elevated relative to productivity
Canada's economic growth expected to average 2¼% over 2025 and 2026
Global economy expected to grow around 3%; US growth strong but slowing; euro area subdued but rising
China economic activity slowed due to weak domestic demand amid property crisis; new stimulus measures expected to stem weakness
why_it_matters: This quarterly monetary policy report provides the Bank of Canada's comprehensive assessment of economic conditions and forward guidance on monetary policy. The 50 bps rate cut signals the Bank's increasing confidence that inflation is sustainably near target, while the detailed outlook and risk analysis inform market expectations for future policy moves. The report covers both domestic and global economic conditions, making it a key reference for understanding Canada's monetary policy trajectory.
macro_context: The Bank of Canada is in an easing cycle, having cut rates by 50 bps at this meeting. Core inflation is forecast to decline gradually, and both upside and downside risks to the inflation outlook are considered equally concerning. The Canadian economy is expected to gradually strengthen with consumer spending and business investment anticipated to pick up as interest rates decline.
